does: This paper introduces CommandSans, a token-level sanitization approach for defending LLM agents against indirect prompt injection attacks. Rather than classifying entire samples as malicious or benign, the method surgically removes instructions directed at AI systems from tool outputs by identifying and filtering individual tokens that constitute commands. The approach leverages instruction-tuning data to train a token-level predictor that distinguishes between executable instructions and benign text, operating as a non-blocking filter that removes detected instructions without requiring context-dependent calibration. The method is evaluated on its ability to strip malicious prompt injections while preserving utility of tool outputs.
connects: This work responds to the growing vulnerability of LLM agents to indirect prompt injection attacks, where malicious instructions embedded in tool outputs can hijack agent behavior. Prior defenses rely on sample-level classification and context-aware safety evaluators, which suffer from high false positive rates and require careful calibration to balance security and usability. CommandSans builds on foundational computer security principles—separating data from code—and applies this principle to the LLM domain. It extends prior work on prompt injection detection and defense by reframing the problem from classification to sanitization, drawing on instruction-tuning data already available in modern LLM training pipelines rather than requiring synthetic attack examples.
enables: This sanitization approach opens pathways toward more practical and deployable defenses for autonomous AI agents in real-world settings. By operating non-contextually and without requiring calibration, the method could enable broader adoption of tool-using agents in security-sensitive domains. Future work could extend token-level sanitization to other injection attack vectors, combine it with complementary defenses for multi-layered protection, or develop more sophisticated instruction-removal techniques that preserve semantic meaning of tool outputs. The approach also suggests new directions for understanding what constitutes an executable instruction in natural language and how instruction tuning fundamentally changes model behavior in ways exploitable through injection attacks.
